title: Made on the Boat
summary: Alfred Moldovan recounts being born in 1921 in the Hungarian Yorkville section of Manhattan to immigrants from a shtetl on the Hungary-Romania-Poland-Czechoslovakia borderlands, his father a Carpathian lumberjack turned bread baker at Pechter Baking, his seamstress mother, and a Bronx childhood so steeped in Yiddish that he did not speak English until public school.

title: He Read the Tag Cover to Cover
summary: Moldovan describes a home with no political life beyond his father dutifully walking union picket lines and reading the Democratic paper the Tag, his mother sewing neckties for extra money, and his own arrival at City College, the Harvard of the working class, where he worked mornings and nights and had no time for politics.

title: Justify Your Existence
summary: Moldovan explains his slim odds of medical school as a Jewish pre-med, his motto to justify your existence, and how after Pearl Harbor he enlisted as a cadet, trained as a radar specialist at Yale, Harvard, and MIT, and shipped to Foggia, Italy with the 455th Bomb Group overseeing two hundred mechanics on B-24s and B-17s.

title: One of Three B Schools
summary: Called home from Italy by a telegram about his sick mother, Moldovan recounts attending Chicago Medical School, one of the schools the Flexner Report had graded a B, and how dean John J. Scheinen single-handedly raised it to Grade A so its graduates, backed by the GI Bill and his own night work, could practice anywhere.

title: Red-Baited Out of Existence
summary: Moldovan describes becoming political in medical school, helping found the Association of Interns and Medical Students and the American Veterans Committee, both red-baited out of existence, and joining classmates to integrate South Chicago beaches and lecture in Illinois high schools to recruit minority and women students into a white male profession.

title: What Should He Be, a Street Cleaner
topic: Family and the Choice to Serve
summary: Moldovan recalls his craftsman father, who wanted only a big house to putter in and was disappointed his son chose East Harlem over Park Avenue, and a family so undramatic about achievement that his sister, asked if she was proud her brother was a doctor, replied, what should he be, a street cleaner.

title: We Need Doctors
topic: From the Medical Society to MCHR
summary: After internship and residencies, Moldovan opened his East Harlem practice serving working-class patients, joined the Physicians Forum and the County Medical Society where he fought a deliberately dysfunctional early Medicaid, and recounts the call from civil rights activists, we need doctors, that led toward the Medical Committee for Human Rights described in the book The Good Doctors.

title: My Wife Took the Bail Money
summary: Moldovan tells of becoming politically active only with the Medical Committee for Human Rights, taking off work so often his accountant warned he would lose his practice, leaving his wife packets of cash as bail money she spent on trips with the kids, and being elected secretary-treasurer who helped raise half a million dollars in 1964.

title: They Found the Bodies in the Dam
summary: Moldovan recounts his first tour in Meridian, Mississippi in August 1964, arriving as the bodies of Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner were found in the dam, helping bring Brooklyn pathologist David Spain to perform a proper autopsy after the official one proved false, and his own shock as an ignorant Northern white, recalling a Florida bus driver who once ordered him out of the back.

title: We Will Be There
topic: The Meaning of Lending a Presence
summary: Moldovan explains that doctors in Mississippi could not legally practice medicine, only lend a presence so people knew they were watched, a phrase he distilled as we will be there, and revisits the demand for David Spain's autopsy after the Meridian pathologist's report on Goodman, Chaney, and Schwerner proved atrocious and untrue.

title: If You Go, I'll Blow Your Head Off
summary: Sent to Selma with Virginia Wells in spring 1965, Moldovan recounts the young people marching on Montgomery without Dr. King's permission, the Committee's makeshift ambulances at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, a sheriff threatening to blow his head off, and how he alone crossed to drag gassed people from houses amid clubbing goons, with John Lewis beaten and concussed.

title: Make Sure You Have Somebody With You
topic: The Second March and a Secret Deal
summary: Moldovan describes speaking at Brown Chapel the night before the second march, joking that anyone knocked unconscious should make sure someone was with them, the column kneeling and praying at the edge of the bridge, and learning only later that King had cut a deal with Washington's Attorney General not to cross.

title: I Was Sure I'd Take a Bullet for Him
summary: Moldovan recounts riding beside Dr. King so that he and Virginia Wells would absorb anything thrown through the car windows, his certainty he would take a bullet, his refusal of paralysis traced to icy wartime roads near Naples, and his affection for King, who laughingly told Andy Young, look who's an atheist, when Moldovan named himself a Jewish atheist.

title: A Moment in History
topic: The Third March and the Gas Mask
summary: Moldovan reflects that he never expected to change the world but played a moment in history, recalls quoting Lenin's One Step Forward and Two Steps Back to King, and that he had left Selma before the final Montgomery march, appearing in a photograph carrying King's gas mask under his coat as the senior physician who assigned himself the role of protecting him.

part: The Committee's Reach and Its Limits
title: Every Leader on One Stage
summary: Moldovan recalls the women in nursing roles, his indifference to the beloved-community Christological language of King and SCLC, and a seminal Committee meeting he helped organize in a Washington auditorium that put the heads of the NAACP, CORE, SCLC, and every major Afro-American leader on one stage at once, the only such gathering he knew.

part: The Committee's Reach and Its Limits
title: That Was Not My Business
summary: Moldovan voices his grim view of an American medical system that spends more and produces less than any other, and recounts opposing war personally while arguing the Committee had no right to take an antiwar stand at the March 1966 Chicago meeting because donors who supported the war funded it, a split with the younger members who, he jokes, thought him antediluvian.

part: The Committee's Reach and Its Limits
title: Two Kinds of Motivation
summary: Moldovan distinguishes two groups in the Committee, people like him moved by civil rights as righting wrongs, and figures like Jack Geiger who built lasting Southern medical organizations, and explains the dichotomy over money he wanted to fund doctors and nurses going South, not to establish clinics, as his active involvement wound down in 1966.

title: I Went to Be There
summary: Moldovan closes with later acts of bearing witness, going to occupied Columbia where he called the police pigs and was rebuked by Margaret Mead, and flying to Israel when the SCUD missiles fell in 1991 to be with family, returning to his rule, justify your existence, and a parting joke that his time is up.
